---
title: "Randomized Controlled Trials"
description: "Random assignment, experimental estimators, compliance, threats to validity, and power analysis."
---

## Motivation {#sec-rct-motivation}

The cleanest solution to the selection problem introduced in [Selection Bias and CIA](00-selection-bias-cia.qmd) is to remove selection altogether: randomly assign treatment.

When an experimenter flips a coin to decide who receives a program and who does not, the assignment mechanism is known, controlled, and independent of potential outcomes. Treated and control groups are comparable in expectation---not just on observed covariates, but on everything, observed and unobserved. No functional-form assumption is needed. No exclusion restriction. No parallel-trends defense. Randomization does the work.

This makes the RCT the benchmark causal design: other identification strategies (IV, DiD, RDD) are evaluated by how close they come to what an experiment would deliver. But experiments are not automatic. Imperfect compliance, differential attrition, spillovers, and underpowered samples can erode the experimental advantage. This chapter formalizes what randomization buys, how to estimate treatment effects with and without full compliance, what threatens experimental validity, and how to design an adequately powered trial.

---

## Random Assignment and Identification {#sec-rct-identification}

For each unit $i$, define potential outcomes $Y_i(1)$ (treated) and $Y_i(0)$ (untreated), and let $D_i \in \{0,1\}$ denote treatment status. The observed outcome is $Y_i = D_i Y_i(1) + (1-D_i)Y_i(0)$.

### Assumptions {#sec-rct-assumptions}

| ID | Name | Statement | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| RCT.1 | **Random assignment** | $(Y_i(0), Y_i(1)) \perp D_i$ | Treatment is independent of potential outcomes; eliminates selection bias |
| RCT.2 | **SUTVA** | $Y_i = Y_i(D_i)$; no interference across units | Each unit's outcome depends only on its own treatment status |
| RCT.3 | **Overlap** | $0 < \mathbb{P}(D_i=1) < 1$ | Both treatment and control are observed |

RCT.1 is the defining feature. It says that the mechanism generating $D_i$ is independent of the potential outcomes. Under simple randomization with probability $p$, each unit has the same chance of treatment regardless of its characteristics.

::: {.callout-warning}
## What Randomization Does *Not* Guarantee

Random assignment guarantees balance *in expectation*, not in every realization. A single draw can produce covariate imbalance by chance. This is why balance tables are reported and stratified randomization is recommended in practice. Randomization also says nothing about external validity: results from one sample may not generalize to a different population or context.
:::

### Identification of the ATE {#sec-rct-ate-identification}

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem 6.1 (Unbiasedness of the Difference in Means)

Under RCT.1--RCT.3, the difference in sample means

$$
\hat{\tau} = \bar{Y}_T - \bar{Y}_C = \frac{1}{n_T}\sum_{i:D_i=1} Y_i - \frac{1}{n_C}\sum_{i:D_i=0} Y_i \tag{6.1}
$$

is an unbiased estimator of the Average Treatment Effect $\text{ATE} = \mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0)]$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## 📐 Expand proof of Theorem 6.1

**Step 1.** By SUTVA, $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid D=1] = \mathbb{E}[Y(1) \mid D=1]$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid D=0] = \mathbb{E}[Y(0) \mid D=0]$.

**Step 2.** By random assignment (RCT.1):

$$
\mathbb{E}[Y(1) \mid D=1] = \mathbb{E}[Y(1)], \qquad \mathbb{E}[Y(0) \mid D=0] = \mathbb{E}[Y(0)].
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\tau}] = \mathbb{E}[Y(1)] - \mathbb{E}[Y(0)] = \text{ATE}.
$$

Compare with the decomposition from [Selection Bias and CIA](00-selection-bias-cia.qmd): the selection bias term $\mathbb{E}[Y(0)\mid D=1] - \mathbb{E}[Y(0)\mid D=0]$ is exactly zero under RCT.1. $\blacksquare$
:::

The standard error of $\hat{\tau}$ under independent sampling is

$$
\text{SE}(\hat{\tau}) = \sqrt{\frac{s_T^2}{n_T} + \frac{s_C^2}{n_C}}, \tag{6.2}
$$

where $s_T^2$ and $s_C^2$ are the sample variances in the treatment and control groups.

---

## Experimental Design {#sec-rct-design}

Knowing that randomization identifies the ATE is not enough. Before running the experiment, three structural decisions must be made: at what *level* to randomize, how to structure the *treatment arms*, and how to *implement* randomization in practice. After randomization, balance must be assessed. This section covers each in turn.

### Level of Randomization {#sec-rct-level}

The first design decision is the unit at which treatment is assigned. The decision tree below guides this choice:

```{=html}
<figure style="margin: 1.5em 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 390" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;max-width:720px;display:block;margin:0 auto;" font-family="'IBM Plex Sans','Helvetica Neue',sans-serif">
  <!-- Root -->
  <rect x="220" y="8" width="280" height="44" rx="8" fill="#f8f9fa" stroke="#4a7fb5" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="360" y="36" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="600" fill="#2d2d2d">Can units be independently assigned?</text>

  <!-- Lines from root -->
  <line x1="290" y1="52" x2="130" y2="100" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="430" y1="52" x2="590" y2="100" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Labels on lines -->
  <text x="195" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#888" font-style="italic">No (spillovers / group-level treatment)</text>
  <text x="530" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#888" font-style="italic">Yes</text>

  <!-- NO → Cluster -->
  <rect x="20" y="100" width="220" height="52" rx="8" fill="#e8f4e8" stroke="#4f9a68" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="130" y="121" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#2d6b3f">Cluster Randomization</text>
  <text x="130" y="139" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#555">randomize groups (villages, schools, clinics)</text>

  <!-- YES → Sample size? -->
  <rect x="500" y="100" width="180" height="38" rx="8" fill="#f8f9fa" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="590" y="124" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#2d2d2d">Sample size?</text>

  <!-- Lines from Sample size -->
  <line x1="540" y1="138" x2="420" y2="196" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="640" y1="138" x2="640" y2="196" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="462" y="166" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-style="italic">Large (N &gt; hundreds)</text>
  <text x="660" y="166" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-style="italic">Small</text>

  <!-- Large → Simple -->
  <rect x="310" y="196" width="220" height="52" rx="8" fill="#e8f0f8" stroke="#4a7fb5" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="420" y="217" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#2d5a8c">Simple Randomization</text>
  <text x="420" y="235" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#555">LLN ensures approximate balance</text>

  <!-- Small → Covariate type? -->
  <rect x="560" y="196" width="150" height="38" rx="8" fill="#f8f9fa" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="635" y="220" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#2d2d2d">Covariate type?</text>

  <!-- Lines from Covariate type -->
  <line x1="600" y1="234" x2="460" y2="300" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="670" y1="234" x2="670" y2="300" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="510" y="268" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-style="italic">Discrete covariates</text>
  <text x="688" y="268" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-style="italic">Continuous / max precision</text>

  <!-- Discrete → Stratified -->
  <rect x="340" y="300" width="240" height="52" rx="8" fill="#fef3e0" stroke="#d4a24c" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="460" y="321" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#8a6420">Stratified Randomization</text>
  <text x="460" y="339" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#555">block on covariates, randomize within strata</text>

  <!-- Continuous → Pairwise -->
  <rect x="590" y="300" width="120" height="52" rx="8" fill="#fce8e8" stroke="#b55d5d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="650" y="321" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#8c3333">Pairwise</text>
  <text x="650" y="339" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#555">pair &amp; flip within</text>
</svg>
<figcaption style="text-align:center;font-size:0.9em;color:#666;margin-top:0.5em;">Choosing the level and method of randomization.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

**Individual-level randomization** assigns each person (or firm, or plot) independently. Every unit is its own observation, so statistical power is maximized. The risk is *spillovers*: if treated and control individuals interact (classmates, neighbors, coworkers), SUTVA breaks and the treatment-control contrast is contaminated.

**Cluster-level randomization** assigns entire groups---villages, schools, clinics---to treatment or control. This avoids within-cluster spillovers by design, but introduces the intra-cluster correlation (ICC) problem: units in the same cluster tend to have similar outcomes, so each additional individual within a cluster adds less information than an independent draw. The effective sample size shrinks by the design effect $\text{DEFF} = 1 + (m-1)\rho$ (see [Power Analysis](#sec-rct-power-cluster)).

**Choosing the unit:** Randomize at the level where the treatment operates or below which spillovers are expected. A village-level water system requires village-level randomization. An individual cash transfer with likely intra-household sharing may require household-level randomization.

#### Simple (Pure) Randomization {#sec-rct-simple}

Each unit is independently assigned to treatment with fixed probability $p$:

$$
D_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Bernoulli}(p). \tag{6.3}
$$

Transparent and easy to verify. The drawback is that group sizes are random ($n_T \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$), and in small samples covariate imbalance can occur by chance. Appropriate when $N$ is large (say $N > 500$) and balance is not a primary concern.

#### Stratified Randomization (Blocking) {#sec-rct-stratified}

Divide the sample into $S$ strata based on pre-treatment covariates (e.g., gender $\times$ region), then randomize independently within each stratum:

$$
D_i \mid \text{Stratum}_s \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Bernoulli}(p_s). \tag{6.4}
$$

This guarantees exact balance on the stratification variables and reduces the variance of $\hat{\tau}$. If the stratification covariates explain a fraction $R_S^2$ of the outcome variance, the precision gain is equivalent to multiplying the sample by $1/(1-R_S^2)$.

Bruhn and McKenzie (2009) show that stratification rarely hurts and often reduces standard errors by $20$--$30\%$. Best practice: stratify on $2$--$3$ variables most correlated with the outcome. Analysis should include strata fixed effects for valid inference (Freedman, 2008).

::: {.callout-warning}
## Too Many Strata

With too many strata relative to sample size, some strata end up with very few units, defeating the purpose. A stratum with a single unit cannot be randomized at all. Keep the number of strata well below $N/4$.
:::

#### Pairwise Matching {#sec-rct-pairwise}

An extreme version of stratification: form pairs of units with similar baseline characteristics, then flip a coin within each pair. One unit gets treatment, the other gets control.

This maximizes balance and precision for a given $N$, but is fragile: if one unit in a pair attrites, the entire pair is lost. Analysis must include pair fixed effects. Most useful with very small samples (e.g., $20$ clusters) and rich baseline data.

#### Cluster Randomization {#sec-rct-cluster-design}

Randomize groups rather than individuals. Necessary when:

- Treatment is inherently group-level (school curriculum, village infrastructure)
- Within-group spillovers would contaminate individual-level randomization
- Administrative or ethical constraints require group-level assignment

The cost is substantial: with $J$ clusters of size $m$ and ICC $\rho$, the effective sample size is only $Jm / [1+(m-1)\rho]$. With fixed budget, increasing the number of clusters is almost always more effective than increasing cluster size. See [Power Analysis](#sec-rct-power-cluster) for formulas.

### Treatment Design {#sec-rct-treatment-design}

The second structural decision is how many treatments to test and how they interact.

**Binary treatment** — a single treatment vs. control — is the simplest design and the default for most RCTs. All power is concentrated on one comparison.

**Multiple arms** — when the question is not just *whether* a program works, but *which version* works best (e.g., different dosages, delivery mechanisms, incentive levels). With $K$ treatment arms, there are $K$ pairwise comparisons against control and $\binom{K}{2}$ across-arm comparisons. Each additional arm requires more sample to maintain power, and multiple comparisons inflate the risk of false positives (see [Pre-analysis Plan](#sec-rct-pap)).

**Factorial design** — cross multiple treatments to test interactions. With two binary treatments $A$ and $B$, four groups arise: pure control, $A$ only, $B$ only, $A + B$.

$$
Y_i = \alpha + \beta_A A_i + \beta_B B_i + \gamma (A_i \times B_i) + \varepsilon_i. \tag{6.5}
$$

The interaction term $\gamma$ tests whether $A$ and $B$ are complements ($\gamma > 0$) or substitutes ($\gamma < 0$). Power for $\gamma$ is typically low---detecting an interaction requires roughly four times the sample needed for a main effect.

### Implementation in Practice {#sec-rct-implementation}

Even with the structure decided, practical constraints shape how randomization is carried out.

#### Oversubscription {#sec-rct-oversubscription}

The most common implementation strategy in development economics. When demand for a program exceeds supply, randomly select beneficiaries from the eligible pool. Those not selected form the control group.

This is ethically clean---everyone who applies has an equal chance---and provides a natural justification for randomization. The estimand is the effect of *receiving the program among applicants*, which may differ from the population ATE if applicants are a selected sample.

#### Phase-In (Stepped Wedge) Design {#sec-rct-phasein}

All units eventually receive treatment; only the *timing* is randomized. Early recipients serve as treatment, later recipients as control. Ethically appealing because no one is permanently denied the intervention, and politically feasible when the implementing organization plans to scale anyway.

Limitations: cannot estimate long-run effects (the control group eventually becomes treated), and anticipation effects may bias short-run estimates. Time trends must be handled carefully---the design is vulnerable to confounding from secular changes.

#### Encouragement Design {#sec-rct-encouragement}

When mandating treatment is unethical or infeasible, randomize an *encouragement*---an information campaign, a subsidy, an invitation---and use it as an instrument for actual treatment take-up.

The randomized encouragement $Z_i$ satisfies the instrument conditions by construction: it is randomly assigned (relevance depends on the encouragement being effective) and plausibly affects outcomes only through take-up (exclusion). The Wald estimator then recovers the LATE for compliers. See [Non-Compliance](#sec-rct-compliance) and [Instrumental Variables](03-iv.qmd).

---

## Power Analysis {#sec-rct-power}

An underpowered trial is wasteful: it cannot detect a real effect even if one exists. Power calculation determines, *before* the experiment begins, how many observations you need to detect the treatment effect with sufficiently high probability. It is arguably the most important step of experimental design, and a required component of grant applications and IRB submissions.

### $p$-value, Significance, and Power {#sec-rct-power-concepts}

Under $H_0: \tau = 0$, the test statistic for the difference in means is

$$
T = \frac{\hat{\tau}}{\text{SE}(\hat{\tau})} \;\overset{H_0}{\sim}\; \mathcal{N}(0,1). \tag{6.6}
$$

The **$p$-value** is the probability, under $H_0$, of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one realized:

$$
p = 2\left[1 - \Phi\left(|T|\right)\right], \tag{6.7}
$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF. We reject $H_0$ at level $\alpha$ when $p < \alpha$, equivalently when $|T| > z_{1-\alpha/2}$.

**Power** is the probability of *correctly* rejecting $H_0$ when the true effect is $\tau = \delta \neq 0$:

$$
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = \mathbb{P}\left(|T| > z_{1-\alpha/2} \;\middle|\; \tau = \delta\right). \tag{6.8}
$$

Under the alternative, $T \sim \mathcal{N}(\delta / \text{SE}, 1)$. For a two-sided test with equal allocation ($n_T = n_C = N/2$) and $\text{SE} = \sigma\sqrt{4/N}$:

$$
\text{Power} = \Phi\left(\frac{\delta}{\sigma\sqrt{4/N}} - z_{1-\alpha/2}\right) + \Phi\left(-\frac{\delta}{\sigma\sqrt{4/N}} - z_{1-\alpha/2}\right). \tag{6.9}
$$

The second term is negligible in practice, so power is approximately $\Phi\left(\delta\sqrt{N}/(2\sigma) - z_{1-\alpha/2}\right)$.

### Sample Size and MDE {#sec-rct-power-formula}

Setting the power expression equal to $1 - \beta$ and solving for $N$ gives the required sample size *per group*:

$$
N_{\text{per group}} = \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{\delta^2}. \tag{6.10}
$$

At $\alpha = 0.05$ and $80\%$ power, $(1.96 + 0.84)^2 \approx 7.84$. A standardized effect of $0.2$ ($\delta = 0.2\sigma$) requires roughly $7.84 / 0.04 = 196$ per group; an effect of $0.5$ requires about $32$.

The same formula, solved in the other direction, gives the **Minimum Detectable Effect** (MDE)---the smallest effect detectable at power $1-\beta$ given a fixed sample size:

$$
\text{MDE} = (z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{4\sigma^2}{N}}. \tag{6.11}
$$

If the MDE exceeds any plausible effect, the study is underpowered and should be redesigned before proceeding. When no prior exists for the effect size, work backwards: fix $N$ from budget constraints, compute MDE, and ask whether that MDE is plausible.

**Cluster adjustment.** When randomization is at the cluster level, within-cluster correlation inflates the required sample. The **design effect** is $\text{DEFF} = 1 + (m - 1)\rho$, where $m$ is the number of units per cluster and $\rho$ is the intra-cluster correlation coefficient (ICC). The effective sample size is $N_{\text{eff}} = N / \text{DEFF}$. Replace $N$ with $N_{\text{eff}}$ in the formulas above.

Even a seemingly small $\rho = 0.05$ with $m = 100$ units per cluster gives $\text{DEFF} \approx 6$: you need roughly six times as many observations as under individual randomization. When $m$ is large, $N_{\text{eff}} \approx J/\rho$ where $J$ is the number of clusters. The binding constraint is always the number of clusters, not cluster size.

**Typical ICC values** in development economics: health outcomes ($0.01$--$0.05$), education test scores ($0.10$--$0.25$), income ($0.02$--$0.10$).

**Intuition: what $\sigma^2_{\text{adj}}$ is doing.** Experimental precision depends on the amount of *effective independent information*. Every adjustment modifies the variance to reflect this:

$$
\sigma^2_{\text{adj}} = \sigma^2 \times \underbrace{(1-\rho^2)}_{\text{strip known noise (ANCOVA)}} \times \underbrace{[1+(m-1)\cdot\text{ICC}]}_{\text{discount redundant information (clustering)}}
$$

ANCOVA *increases* effective information by explaining away predictable noise; clustering *decreases* it because correlated observations carry less independent signal. When both are present the effects multiply.

### Quick-Reference Formulas {#sec-rct-power-quick}

Define $Z_{\text{sum}} = z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}$. The two core formulas for equal-sized groups ($n$ per group) are:

$$
\boxed{\text{MDE} = Z_{\text{sum}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2\sigma^2}{n}}}, \qquad \boxed{n = \frac{2\,(Z_{\text{sum}} \cdot \sigma)^2}{\text{MDE}^2}}.
$$

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Expand: $Z_{\text{sum}}$ lookup table and scenario adjustments

**$Z_{\text{sum}}$ lookup table**

|  | Power 80% | Power 90% | Power 95% |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha = 0.05$ | 2.80 | 3.24 | 3.60 |
| $\alpha = 0.01$ | 3.42 | 3.86 | 4.22 |
| $\alpha = 0.10$ | 2.49 | 2.93 | 3.29 |

**Scenario adjustments: replace $\sigma^2$ with $\sigma^2_{\text{adj}}$**

All adjustments preserve the formula structure---only the variance term changes:

$$
\text{MDE} = Z_{\text{sum}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2\,\sigma^2_{\text{adj}}}{n}}, \qquad n = \frac{2\,Z_{\text{sum}}^2 \cdot \sigma^2_{\text{adj}}}{\text{MDE}^2}.
$$

| Scenario | $\sigma^2_{\text{adj}}$ | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Baseline | $\sigma^2$ | Raw outcome variance |
| ANCOVA | $\sigma^2(1-\rho^2)$ | $\rho$ = correlation between $Y$ and covariate |
| Cluster | $\sigma^2[1+(m-1)\cdot\text{ICC}]$ | $m$ = cluster size |
| Cluster + ANCOVA | $\sigma^2(1-\rho^2)[1+(m-1)\cdot\text{ICC}]$ | Both adjustments multiply |
| Unequal allocation | Replace $\frac{2}{n}$ with $\frac{1}{n \cdot \pi(1-\pi)}$ | $\pi$ = treatment share |

**Expanded sample size formulas by scenario:**

| Scenario | $n$ per group |
|---|---|
| Baseline | $\dfrac{2\,Z_{\text{sum}}^2\,\sigma^2}{\text{MDE}^2}$ |
| ANCOVA | $\dfrac{2\,Z_{\text{sum}}^2\,\sigma^2(1-\rho^2)}{\text{MDE}^2}$ |
| Cluster | $\dfrac{2\,Z_{\text{sum}}^2\,\sigma^2[1+(m-1)\cdot\text{ICC}]}{\text{MDE}^2}$ |
| Cluster + ANCOVA | $\dfrac{2\,Z_{\text{sum}}^2\,\sigma^2(1-\rho^2)[1+(m-1)\cdot\text{ICC}]}{\text{MDE}^2}$ |

:::

### The Seven-Step Workflow {#sec-rct-power-workflow}

**Step 1. Choose the primary outcome.** Power calculation should be driven by the single outcome you care about most. With multiple primary outcomes, each needs its own power calculation and multiple testing corrections increase sample size requirements.

**Step 2. Estimate outcome variance ($\sigma^2$).** Sources: baseline data, pilot study, or published studies using similar populations and outcomes. This step is critical---an inaccurate variance estimate cascades through the entire calculation.

**Step 3. Set the expected effect size ($\delta$).** The most judgment-intensive step. Sources: effect sizes from similar interventions in the literature, your own pilot results, or the minimum policy-relevant effect. If you have no prior, work backwards: fix $N$ (from budget constraints), compute the MDE, and ask whether that MDE is plausible.

**Step 4. Set $\alpha$ and desired power.** Convention: $\alpha = 0.05$, power $= 0.80$. High-stakes evaluations sometimes use power $= 0.90$, which increases the required sample by roughly $30\%$.

**Step 5. Adjust for design features.**

- *Cluster randomization*: multiply $\sigma^2$ by $\text{DEFF}$
- *Covariate adjustment (ANCOVA)*: multiply $\sigma^2$ by $(1 - \rho^2)$
- *Attrition*: divide the computed $N$ by $(1 - a)$ where $a$ is the expected attrition rate
- *Multiple treatment arms*: each pairwise comparison needs adequate power; total $N$ scales accordingly

**Step 6. Compute.** Combine the formula with adjustments. Suppose $\sigma = 10$, $\delta = 2$, $\alpha = 0.05$, power $= 0.80$:

$$
N_{\text{per group}} = \frac{(1.96 + 0.84)^2 \times 100}{4} = \frac{784}{4} = 196.
$$

Total sample: $392$. With a baseline covariate $R^2 = 0.3$: $196 \times 0.7 = 137$ per group. With $15\%$ attrition: $137 / 0.85 \approx 161$ per group.

**Step 7. Sensitivity analysis.** Never report a single number. Plot a power curve (sample size on the horizontal axis, power on the vertical) under several effect size scenarios (e.g., $\delta = 1.5, 2.0, 2.5$). This lets you, your PI, and funders assess: *if the true effect is smaller than expected, do we still have adequate power?*

### Worked Example: Mobile Savings in Kenya {#sec-rct-power-example}

You are designing an RCT to evaluate whether SMS savings reminders increase smallholder savings in Kenya. Primary outcome: savings balance at 6 months. From pilot data: $\sigma = 3000$ KES, village-level $\rho = 0.08$. Literature on similar nudge interventions suggests effects of $0.15$--$0.25\sigma$; you conservatively use $\delta = 0.15\sigma = 450$ KES. Plan: cluster randomize at the village level, survey $m = 30$ households per village.

**Baseline (no clustering):**

$$
N_{\text{per group}} = \frac{7.84 \times 3000^2}{450^2} = \frac{7.84 \times 9{,}000{,}000}{202{,}500} \approx 348.
$$

**Adjust for clustering:** $\text{DEFF} = 1 + 29 \times 0.08 = 3.32$. Adjusted $N_{\text{per group}} = 348 \times 3.32 \approx 1{,}156$, i.e., $1{,}156 / 30 \approx 39$ villages per arm, $78$ total.

**Adjust for ANCOVA:** controlling baseline savings with $R^2 = 0.4$ reduces to $39 \times 0.6 \approx 24$ villages per arm, $48$ total.

**Adjust for attrition:** with $10\%$ expected attrition, $48 / 0.9 \approx 53$ villages total.

This produces a well-justified sample size recommendation for a grant proposal.

### Strategies to Increase Power {#sec-rct-power-strategies}

1. **More clusters** (not more units per cluster) in cluster-randomized trials
2. **Baseline covariates** via ANCOVA to reduce residual variance
3. **Stratified randomization** on variables correlated with the outcome
4. **Multiple follow-up rounds** for panel-data designs
5. **More precise outcome measures** (administrative data over self-reports)

---

## Pre-analysis Plan {#sec-rct-pap}

A **pre-analysis plan** (PAP) is a document filed before endline data are collected that specifies the hypotheses, outcome variables, subgroup analyses, and regression specifications. The purpose is to prevent *specification searching*---the temptation to mine the data until a significant result appears.

Key elements:

- **Primary outcomes** and how they are constructed (indices, transformations)
- **Primary specifications** (with and without controls, heterogeneity cuts)
- **Multiple hypothesis testing correction**: with $K$ hypotheses, the Bonferroni correction tests each at $\alpha/K$; the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure controls the false discovery rate (FDR) at $\alpha$ and is less conservative
- **Rules for handling attrition, outliers, and missing data**

PAPs are typically registered on the AEA RCT Registry or EGAP. They do not prevent exploratory analysis---but exploratory results should be clearly labelled as such.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Multiple Hypothesis Testing

With $20$ outcomes tested at the $5\%$ level, expect one spurious rejection by chance. The Bonferroni correction ($\alpha' = 0.05/20 = 0.0025$) is simple but conservative. The Benjamini-Hochberg procedure is standard practice: rank the $p$-values, and reject all hypotheses whose $p$-value $\leq (k/K) \cdot \alpha$, where $k$ is the rank and $K$ is the total number of tests.
:::

---

## Assessment after Randomization {#sec-rct-balance}

After randomization, report a balance table comparing baseline covariates across treatment and control. Standard practice:

- $t$-tests for each covariate separately
- An $F$-test (joint test): regress $D_i$ on all baseline covariates and test joint significance
- Normalized differences (Imbens and Rubin, 2015): more informative than $t$-tests in large samples where even trivial imbalances become "significant"

$$
\Delta_x = \frac{\bar{X}_T - \bar{X}_C}{\sqrt{(S_T^2 + S_C^2)/2}} \tag{6.13}
$$

where $S_T^2$ and $S_C^2$ are the sample variances in treatment and control. Unlike $t$-statistics, $\Delta_x$ does not mechanically grow with $N$. A rule of thumb: $|\Delta_x| > 0.25$ suggests meaningful imbalance that warrants covariate adjustment.

If imbalance is found: do *not* re-randomize until balance is achieved (this invalidates inference). Instead, include the imbalanced covariates as controls in the analysis and report the balance table transparently. With $20$ covariates tested at the $5\%$ level, expect one spurious "significant" difference by chance.

---

## Estimation Strategies {#sec-rct-estimation}

### Difference in Means via Regression {#sec-rct-regression}

The simplest regression for an RCT is

$$
Y_i = \alpha + \tau D_i + \varepsilon_i. \tag{6.14}
$$

The OLS estimate of $\tau$ is numerically identical to $\bar{Y}_T - \bar{Y}_C$. The regression framework is convenient because it extends naturally to covariates and produces standard errors directly.

### Regression Adjustment for Precision {#sec-rct-covariate-adjustment}

Adding pre-treatment covariates $X_i$ to the regression does not change the unbiasedness of $\hat{\tau}$ (because $D$ is independent of $X$ by design), but it reduces the residual variance and tightens confidence intervals:

$$
Y_i = \alpha + \tau D_i + X_i'\beta + \varepsilon_i. \tag{6.15}
$$

Lin (2013) shows that including treatment-covariate interactions $D_i \times (X_i - \bar{X})$ provides robust efficiency gains without relying on correct specification of the covariate model. If randomization was stratified, Freedman (2008) recommends including strata fixed effects for correct inference.

### ANCOVA {#sec-rct-ancova}

When a baseline measure of the outcome $Y_{i,\text{pre}}$ is available, the ANCOVA specification

$$
Y_{i,\text{post}} = \alpha + \tau D_i + \gamma Y_{i,\text{pre}} + \varepsilon_i \tag{6.16}
$$

controls for the pre-treatment level. McKenzie (2012) shows that ANCOVA is generally more efficient than a difference-in-differences approach when the autocorrelation of the outcome is moderate. The precision gain is equivalent to increasing the sample size by a factor of $1/(1-R^2)$, where $R^2$ is the share of outcome variance explained by the baseline measure.

---

## Non-Compliance: ITT, TOT, and LATE {#sec-rct-compliance}

In practice, assignment does not always equal receipt. Some units assigned to treatment never take it up (*never-takers*); some assigned to control access treatment anyway (*always-takers*). Let $Z_i$ denote random assignment and $D_i$ denote actual treatment received.

### Compliance Types {#sec-rct-compliance-types}

Under the monotonicity assumption---no unit does the opposite of its assignment---the population partitions into four latent groups:

| Type | $D_i$ if $Z_i=1$ | $D_i$ if $Z_i=0$ | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Complier** | 1 | 0 | Takes treatment if and only if assigned |
| **Always-taker** | 1 | 1 | Takes treatment regardless |
| **Never-taker** | 0 | 0 | Refuses treatment regardless |
| **Defier** | 0 | 1 | Does the opposite; ruled out by monotonicity |

### ITT, TOT, and the Wald Estimator {#sec-rct-itt-tot}

The **Intention-to-Treat** (ITT) effect compares outcomes by *assignment*, regardless of compliance:

$$
\text{ITT} = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=1] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=0]. \tag{6.17}
$$

ITT is always identified under randomization and measures the policy-relevant effect of *offering* a program. With imperfect compliance, ITT is attenuated toward zero relative to the treatment effect on those who actually comply.

The **Treatment-on-the-Treated** effect for compliers is recovered by the **Wald estimator**:

$$
\hat{\tau}_{\text{LATE}} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=1] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=0]}{\mathbb{E}[D \mid Z=1] - \mathbb{E}[D \mid Z=0]} = \frac{\text{ITT}}{\text{First Stage}}. \tag{6.18}
$$

This is numerically identical to 2SLS using $Z$ as an instrument for $D$ (see [Instrumental Variables](03-iv.qmd)).

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem 6.2 (Wald Estimator Identifies LATE)

Under RCT.1--RCT.3 plus:

- **Relevance**: $\mathbb{E}[D \mid Z=1] \neq \mathbb{E}[D \mid Z=0]$ (assignment affects take-up)
- **Exclusion**: $Z$ affects $Y$ only through $D$
- **Monotonicity**: $D_i(1) \geq D_i(0)$ for all $i$ (no defiers)

the Wald estimator identifies the Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE):

$$
\hat{\tau}_{\text{LATE}} \xrightarrow{p} \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) \mid \text{complier}]. \tag{6.19}
$$
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## 📐 Expand proof of Theorem 6.2

**Step 1.** By random assignment, the ITT numerator is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=1] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=0] = \mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid \text{complier}] \cdot \mathbb{P}(\text{complier}).
$$

To see this, decompose by compliance type. Always-takers have $D(1) = D(0) = 1$, so their $Y$ is the same under $Z=1$ and $Z=0$. Never-takers have $D(1) = D(0) = 0$, same logic. Only compliers switch treatment status when $Z$ changes, contributing $\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid \text{complier}]$ to the ITT.

**Step 2.** The first stage is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[D \mid Z=1] - \mathbb{E}[D \mid Z=0] = \mathbb{P}(\text{complier}).
$$

Again, only compliers change $D$ in response to $Z$.

**Step 3.** Dividing:

$$
\frac{\text{ITT}}{\text{First Stage}} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid \text{complier}] \cdot \mathbb{P}(\text{complier})}{\mathbb{P}(\text{complier})} = \mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid \text{complier}].
$$

$\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Weak First Stage

When compliance is low, the denominator of the Wald estimator is small, making LATE estimates noisy and potentially biased in finite samples. If the first-stage F-statistic is below 10, treat IV/LATE results with caution and consider reporting ITT as the primary result. See [IV diagnostics](03-iv.qmd#sec-iv-weak) for more on weak instruments.
:::

---

## Threats to Internal Validity {#sec-rct-threats-internal}

Internal validity asks: *does the estimated effect reflect the true causal impact within this experiment?* Even a well-randomized trial can fail on this dimension.

### Attrition {#sec-rct-attrition}

Units that drop out between baseline and endline break the experimental balance if attrition is correlated with treatment assignment (*differential attrition*).

**Detection:**

- Compare attrition rates across treatment and control
- Compare baseline characteristics of attritors vs.\ non-attritors by group
- Use Lee (2009) bounds to provide worst-case treatment effect estimates under attrition

**Prevention:** Track participants aggressively; keep surveys short; collect multiple contact methods at baseline; provide incentives for follow-up.

### Spillovers and SUTVA Violations {#sec-rct-spillovers}

If treated units affect the outcomes of control units---through information sharing, resource transfers, or market-level price changes---SUTVA (RCT.2) is violated and the simple treated-control comparison underestimates or overestimates the true effect.

**Design solutions:**

- Cluster-randomize at a level above which spillovers are unlikely (villages, schools, markets)
- Create buffer zones between treated and control areas
- Randomize treatment *intensity* at the cluster level to directly measure spillover effects

Miguel and Kremer (2004) document substantial deworming spillovers through school-level health externalities---a canonical example of why SUTVA cannot be taken for granted.

### Hawthorne and John Henry Effects {#sec-rct-behavioral}

- **Hawthorne effect**: Treatment group changes behavior because of being observed, not because of the treatment itself
- **John Henry effect**: Control group compensates by exerting extra effort

Both bias the treatment-control contrast in opposite directions: the Hawthorne effect inflates the estimated effect (treatment group improves for non-treatment reasons), while the John Henry effect attenuates it (control group performs better than it otherwise would).

### Placebo Effect {#sec-rct-placebo}

Subjects may improve simply because they *believe* they are receiving an effective treatment, regardless of whether the treatment has any real impact. In medical trials this is well-known, but it also arises in social experiments: participants in a training program may exert more effort because they feel invested in, not because of the training content itself.

**Mitigation:** Use a **placebo arm**---a group that receives an intervention that looks and feels like the real treatment but omits the active ingredient. For example, if testing a financial literacy curriculum, the placebo group receives an equally intensive but unrelated curriculum (health, general knowledge). The treatment effect is then estimated as the difference between the active treatment arm and the placebo arm, isolating the effect of the content from the effect of participation itself.

### Experimenter Demand Effects and Surveyor Bias {#sec-rct-experimenter}

**Experimenter demand effects** arise when subjects infer what the experimenter "wants" and adjust their behavior or survey responses accordingly. If subjects in the treatment group know the experimenter expects the program to work, they may over-report positive outcomes.

**Surveyor bias** occurs when enumerators know which subjects are treated and unconsciously (or consciously) record data differently---probing more for positive responses in the treatment group or coding ambiguous answers favorably.

**Mitigation:**

- **Blinding (single-blind):** Subjects do not know whether they are in treatment or control. Often difficult in social experiments where the treatment is visible (e.g., cash transfers).
- **Double-blinding:** Neither subjects nor data collectors know assignment status. Feasible when outcomes are measured via administrative data or when treatment and placebo are indistinguishable to surveyors.
- Use **administrative data** rather than self-reported outcomes where possible
- Standardize survey protocols and train enumerators to follow scripts strictly

---

## External Validity {#sec-rct-threats-external}

External validity asks: *does the result generalize beyond this experiment?* An RCT with perfect internal validity may still produce results that are specific to the study population, context, or implementation.

### Site and Population Selection {#sec-rct-site-selection}

Most RCTs are conducted in sites that are convenient, cooperative, or have implementing partners---not sites that are representative of the broader population of policy interest. Results from an NGO-led program in western Kenya may not transfer to government-run programs in South Asia.

**Partial remedies:**

- **Multi-site trials**: Run the same intervention in multiple locations and estimate site-specific effects. Heterogeneity across sites directly measures the threat.
- Report baseline characteristics of the study sample alongside national or regional benchmarks so readers can assess similarity.

### Scalability and General Equilibrium Effects {#sec-rct-scalability}

A program that works at small scale may fail at scale for several reasons:

- **Implementation quality**: NGO-run pilots with highly motivated staff may not replicate under government bureaucracy
- **General equilibrium effects**: A job training program that raises wages for $500$ participants may depress wages for untrained workers if scaled to $500{,}000$ participants
- **Cream-skimming**: Pilot programs often enroll the most motivated or accessible beneficiaries; marginal participants at scale may respond differently

### Hawthorne Effects on External Validity {#sec-rct-hawthorne-external}

The Hawthorne effect threatens external validity as well as internal validity: if participants behave differently *because they are in a study*, the measured effect will not persist once the study ends and the extra attention disappears. Using longer follow-up periods and administrative (rather than survey-based) outcomes helps assess whether effects survive beyond the experimental period.

---

## Interactive Simulation {#sec-rct-simulation}

The app below simulates a randomized trial with adjustable sample size, treatment effect, and compliance rate. With perfect compliance, the simple difference in means recovers the ATE. As compliance falls, the ITT is attenuated toward zero and the Wald/IV estimator recovers the LATE for compliers.

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 620
#| echo: false

# Simulation: RCT with compliance, ITT vs LATE

from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

app_ui = ui.page_fillable(
    ui.layout_sidebar(
        ui.sidebar(
            ui.input_slider("n", "Sample size (n)", min=200, max=5000, value=1000, step=100),
            ui.input_slider("tau", "True LATE (effect on compliers)", min=0.0, max=3.0, value=1.5, step=0.1),
            ui.input_slider("comply", "Compliance rate", min=0.1, max=1.0, value=0.7, step=0.05),
            ui.input_action_button("resample", "Resample", class_="btn-primary w-100 mt-2"),
            ui.hr(),
            ui.markdown(
                "**Try:** Lower compliance to see ITT shrink toward zero "
                "while the Wald/IV estimator still recovers the LATE."
            ),
            width=320,
        ),
        ui.output_plot("rct_plot"),
    ),
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @reactive.calc
    def run_trial():
        input.resample()
        n = input.n()
        tau = input.tau()
        comply_rate = input.comply()

        # Random assignment (50-50)
        z = np.random.binomial(1, 0.5, n)

        # Compliance: compliers take up if assigned; never-takers don't
        is_complier = np.random.binomial(1, comply_rate, n)
        d = z * is_complier  # one-sided non-compliance

        # Potential outcomes (constant effect for compliers)
        y0 = np.random.normal(5, 2, n)
        y1 = y0 + tau * is_complier  # only compliers benefit
        y = d * y1 + (1 - d) * y0

        return z, d, y, tau

    @render.plot
    def rct_plot():
        z, d, y, tau_true = run_trial()

        # ITT: compare by assignment
        itt = y[z == 1].mean() - y[z == 0].mean()

        # First stage: compliance rate
        first_stage = d[z == 1].mean() - d[z == 0].mean()

        # Wald / IV estimator
        wald = itt / first_stage if abs(first_stage) > 0.001 else np.nan

        # Naive (compare by actual treatment)
        if d.sum() > 0 and (1 - d).sum() > 0:
            naive = y[d == 1].mean() - y[d == 0].mean()
        else:
            naive = np.nan

        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.3))

        # Panel 1: estimator comparison
        labels = ["ITT", "Wald/IV\n(LATE)", "True\nLATE"]
        vals = [itt, wald, tau_true]
        colors = ["#4a7fb5", "#4f9a68", "#2d2d2d"]
        axes[0].bar(labels, vals, color=colors, alpha=0.9, width=0.5)
        axes[0].axhline(tau_true, color="#2d2d2d", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.2)
        axes[0].set_ylabel("Estimated effect", fontsize=11)
        axes[0].set_title("ITT vs LATE", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")

        info = (
            "True LATE     = {:.3f}\n"
            "ITT           = {:.3f}\n"
            "First stage   = {:.3f}\n"
            "Wald (LATE)   = {:.3f}"
        ).format(tau_true, itt, first_stage, wald)
        axes[0].text(
            0.03, 0.97, info,
            transform=axes[0].transAxes, fontsize=8.5, va="top",
            fontfamily="monospace",
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.4", facecolor="white",
                      edgecolor="#cccccc", alpha=0.95),
        )

        # Panel 2: outcome distributions by assignment
        bins = np.linspace(y.min(), y.max(), 35)
        axes[1].hist(y[z == 0], bins=bins, alpha=0.45, density=True,
                     label="Y | Z=0 (control)", color="#7aa6d1")
        axes[1].hist(y[z == 1], bins=bins, alpha=0.45, density=True,
                     label="Y | Z=1 (assigned)", color="#d98b8b")
        axes[1].set_xlabel("Outcome Y", fontsize=11)
        axes[1].set_ylabel("Density", fontsize=11)
        axes[1].set_title("Outcomes by Assignment", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
        axes[1].legend(fontsize=8.5)

        plt.tight_layout()
        return fig

app = App(app_ui, server)
```

---

## Summary {#sec-rct-summary}

| Result | Required Assumptions | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Theorem 6.1** (Difference in means) | RCT.1--RCT.3 (random assignment, SUTVA, overlap) | $\hat{\tau} = \bar{Y}_T - \bar{Y}_C$ is unbiased for ATE |
| Regression adjustment | RCT.1--RCT.3 + pre-treatment covariates | Same $\hat{\tau}$, tighter confidence intervals |
| ANCOVA | RCT.1--RCT.3 + baseline outcome measure | Precision gain equivalent to $1/(1-R^2)$ increase in $N$ |
| **Theorem 6.2** (Wald/LATE) | RCT.1--RCT.3 + relevance, exclusion, monotonicity | ITT / first stage identifies LATE for compliers |
| Cluster design effect | Cluster randomization with ICC $\rho$ | Effective $N$ reduced by factor $1 + (m-1)\rho$ |

The experimental ideal is simple: randomize, compare means, done. Real experiments are messier. Compliance is imperfect, attrition is differential, spillovers violate SUTVA, and samples are finite. Each complication has a well-defined response---IV for non-compliance, Lee bounds for attrition, cluster randomization for spillovers, power analysis for sample design---but none is automatic. The experimenter's job is to anticipate these threats at the design stage, not to fix them after the data are collected.

When full randomization is not feasible, the designs in [Instrumental Variables](03-iv.qmd), [Difference-in-Differences](04-did.qmd), [Regression Discontinuity](05-rdd.qmd), and [Synthetic Control](08-synthetic-control.qmd) offer alternatives that trade the experimental ideal for identification under weaker but still explicit assumptions.
